questions.text: What does 'Distance Marketing' of insurance refer to?
options: Selling policies without face-to-face interaction, such as via telephone, internet, or email
Agents traveling long distances to meet clients
questions.explanation: Distance marketing involves selling insurance products over the phone, internet, or SMS, where the agent and the customer do not meet physically.

questions.text: What is the 'Free Look Period' for life insurance policies purchased through Distance Marketing or Electronic mode?
questions.explanation: While the standard Free Look Period is 15 days, IRDAI grants an extended 30-day Free Look Period for policies bought electronically or through distance marketing.

questions.text: How is the 'Paid-up Value' of a traditional life insurance policy calculated?
options: (Number of premiums paid / Total premiums payable) x Sum Assured
questions.explanation: The proportionate Paid-up Value is calculated by dividing the number of premiums actually paid by the total number of premiums payable, multiplied by the original Sum Assured.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a 'Valid Discharge' for an insurance company?
options: Paying the claim money to the agent
Paying the claim money to the legally entitled person (Nominee/Assignee/Appointee) who signs a discharge voucher
Refunding the premium to the bank
Paying the money to the Ombudsman
questions.explanation: A valid discharge means the insurer has successfully fulfilled its contractual obligation by paying the right amount to the legally authorized person, releasing the company from further liability.

questions.text: If an insurance agent leaves one insurance company, can they immediately join another life insurance company?
options: No, they must surrender their agency, get an NOC (No Objection Certificate), and then apply for a new appointment
Yes, they can work for both simultaneously
No, they are banned for 5 years
questions.explanation: An agent can only represent one life insurance company at a time. To switch, they must resign, obtain an NOC, and follow the regulatory transfer process.

questions.text: Which type of life insurance policy allows the policyholder to increase or decrease the premium and death benefit during the policy term?
questions.explanation: Universal Life Insurance (and sometimes specific ULIPs) offers flexibility, allowing the policyholder to adjust the premium amount and death benefit as their financial situation changes.

questions.text: What does a 'Survival Benefit' in a Child Plan typically fund?
options: The child's educational milestones like college admission
questions.explanation: Child plans are structured to release periodic survival benefits (lump sums) exactly when the child reaches specific ages (e.g., 18, 21) to fund higher education.

questions.text: In insurance, what does 'Utmost Good Faith' require from the insurer's side?
options: To pay claims without asking questions
To disclose all policy terms, conditions, and charges transparently to the customer
To offer discounts to healthy customers
questions.explanation: Just as the proposer must disclose health facts, the insurer must also act in good faith by clearly explaining all rules, charges, and exclusions of the policy.

questions.text: Which legal document transfers the ownership of a life insurance policy as a gift to a family member?
options: Absolute Assignment deed executed out of natural love and affection
questions.explanation: To gift a policy, an 'Absolute Assignment' is used. It transfers all rights to the relative completely, usually stated 'out of natural love and affection'.

questions.text: What happens if a policyholder stops paying premiums on a ULIP before the 5-year lock-in period ends?
options: The policy is cancelled and funds are lost
The fund value is immediately refunded
The fund value is moved to a 'Discontinued Policy Fund' and paid out only after the 5-year lock-in completes
questions.explanation: If a ULIP is discontinued early, the money is parked in a Discontinued Policy Fund (earning a minimum guaranteed interest) and is accessible only after the 5-year lock-in period ends.

questions.text: What does 'TDS' stand for in the context of taxable insurance payouts?
questions.explanation: If a maturity claim does not qualify for Section 10(10D) exemption, the insurance company is required by law to deduct TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) before paying the customer.

questions.text: Underwriting is primarily based on the principle of:
questions.explanation: Underwriting ensures equity. It ensures that a healthy 30-year-old doesn't pay the same premium as an unhealthy 50-year-old, keeping the system fair.

questions.text: What is the consequence of 'Non-Disclosure' of a material fact by the policyholder?
options: The insurer will increase the sum assured
The policy may be declared void, and a future claim can be repudiated
questions.explanation: Hiding material facts (like a pre-existing disease) breaches the principle of Utmost Good Faith, giving the insurer the right to cancel the policy and reject claims.

questions.text: In a 'Micro-Insurance' life policy, the maximum term of the policy is generally capped at:
questions.explanation: As per IRDAI's Micro-Insurance regulations, to keep the products simple and short-to-medium term for the rural sector, the maximum policy term is generally 15 years.

questions.text: When a life insurance policy is revived, what is the 'Period of Contestability' for the revived portion?
options: It restarts, meaning the insurer has another 3-year window to contest the policy for fraud or misrepresentation
questions.explanation: When a lapsed policy is revived by submitting a new declaration of health, the 3-year indisputability clock (Section 45) restarts from the date of revival.

questions.text: Which document is generally NOT required for settling a Maturity Claim?
questions.explanation: A maturity claim is paid when the policyholder is alive. Therefore, a death certificate is entirely irrelevant for a maturity settlement.

questions.text: An agent discovers that a prospect has a dangerous hobby like car racing. What should the agent do?
options: Tell the prospect to hide it in the form
Ignore it as it is not a daily job
Record it accurately in the Agent's Confidential Report (ACR) and proposal form
questions.explanation: The agent is the primary underwriter. It is their ethical duty to accurately report any hazardous hobbies to the company so the risk can be properly assessed.

questions.text: What does an 'Exclusion' clause do in an Accidental Death Benefit rider?
options: Lists specific situations (like self-inflicted injury or participation in riots) where the accidental benefit will NOT be paid
questions.explanation: Exclusions define the boundaries of coverage. Standard accidental riders exclude deaths caused by suicide, drug abuse, or involvement in criminal acts.

questions.text: What is the primary role of a 'Reinsurer'?
options: To provide insurance to direct insurance companies to help them absorb huge potential losses
questions.explanation: A reinsurer assumes a portion of the risk from the primary insurance company, ensuring the primary company doesn't go bankrupt in case of massive or multiple claims.

questions.text: Which authority issues the PAN (Permanent Account Number) card, which is a key KYC document?
questions.explanation: The PAN card is a unique 10-character alphanumeric identifier issued by the Income Tax Department of India.

questions.text: What is 'Adverse Selection' commonly a result of?
options: Asymmetric Information (the buyer knows more about their bad health than the seller)
Too many branches of the insurer
questions.explanation: Adverse selection happens because of asymmetric information—when a prospect conceals critical health data that the insurer doesn't know about.

questions.text: What happens if the age of the policyholder was overstated (they are actually younger than declared) at the time of proposal?
options: The insurer will refund the excess premium collected without interest
questions.explanation: If age was overstated, the insured paid a higher premium than necessary. The insurer will adjust the records and refund the excess premium paid.

questions.text: Under Section 39, can a policyholder nominate a trust or an institution instead of a person?
options: No, only individuals can be nominated
Yes, a recognized trust or institution can be named as a nominee
Only a bank can be nominated
Only the government can be nominated
questions.explanation: Yes, Section 39 allows for the nomination of individuals as well as registered trusts or institutions to receive the policy proceeds.

questions.text: Which type of assignment is usually executed when a policy is pledged as collateral security for a bank loan?
questions.explanation: Conditional Assignment transfers rights to the bank to secure the loan. Once the loan is fully repaid, the condition is met, and rights revert back to the policyholder.

questions.text: Which type of life insurance is strictly meant for wealth creation and market-linked returns?
questions.explanation: ULIPs are specifically designed for customers looking for market-linked wealth creation along with life insurance protection.

questions.text: What is an 'Annuity for life with a Return of Purchase Price'?
options: The annuitant gets a pension for life, and after death, the initial invested amount is refunded to the nominee
The annuitant gets back their money after 1 year
A pension that stops after 10 years
A term plan that returns the premium
questions.explanation: This is a popular pension option where the annuitant enjoys lifelong income, and upon their demise, the principal amount (purchase price) is given to their legal heirs.

questions.text: If a claim is submitted to the Insurance Ombudsman, within how much time must the Ombudsman pass an award?
options: 3 months from the date of receipt of all requirements from the complainant
questions.explanation: The Insurance Ombudsman is mandated to resolve the dispute and pass an award within 3 months after all the necessary documents are filed by the complainant.

questions.text: What does a 'Level Premium' system ensure for the policyholder?
options: The premium increases by 5% every year
The premium remains exactly the same every year for the entire policy term
The premium decreases as the person gets older
The premium fluctuates with the stock market
questions.explanation: In a level premium system, the insurer averages out the increasing mortality risk over the term, allowing the policyholder to pay a constant, predictable premium every year.

questions.text: A group insurance policy is usually issued to an employer. What is the document given to the individual employees called?
questions.explanation: The employer holds the 'Master Policy', while each covered employee is issued a 'Certificate of Insurance' as proof of their coverage under the group scheme.

questions.text: Which phase of money laundering involves moving illicit funds around the financial system to hide their origin?
questions.explanation: Layering is the complex process of creating multiple financial transactions (moving money between accounts or buying/canceling insurance) to obscure the source of the dirty money.

questions.text: In insurance, what does 'Solvency Ratio' measure?
options: The number of agents a company has
The ability of an insurance company to meet its long-term financial obligations (claims) to policyholders
The amount of tax the company pays
questions.explanation: The solvency ratio indicates financial health. IRDAI requires companies to maintain a minimum solvency ratio of 150% to ensure they can pay out all claims.

questions.text: Which document signifies the end of the insurance contract and releases the insurer from further liabilities after a claim is paid?
questions.explanation: A Discharge Voucher (or Discharge Receipt) is signed by the claimant acknowledging that they have received the claim money and the contract is fully settled.

questions.text: Who bears the risk of lower-than-expected mortality (people living longer) in an Annuity product?
questions.explanation: In an annuity, the insurer promises to pay as long as the person lives. If annuitants live longer than statistically expected, the insurance company bears the financial loss.

questions.text: What does 'Foreclosure' of a life insurance policy mean?
options: The policyholder voluntarily cancels the policy
The insurer terminates the policy because the outstanding loan and interest exceed the policy's surrender value
questions.explanation: When a policyholder takes a loan and defaults on interest, and the total debt grows larger than the cash value of the policy, the insurer forecloses (closes) it to recover the debt.

questions.text: What is the primary role of an 'Actuary' in a life insurance company?
options: To sell policies in the field
To use mathematics and statistics to calculate mortality rates, price products, and ensure financial stability
questions.explanation: Actuaries are highly specialized professionals who evaluate financial risks, calculate premium rates, and determine the reserves needed to pay future claims.

questions.text: Under Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, any payout from a life insurance policy is tax-free, EXCEPT for:
options: Payouts from a Keyman Insurance policy
questions.explanation: Section 10(10D) exempts standard life policies. However, payouts from a Keyman Insurance policy are treated as business income and are fully taxable.

questions.text: Which type of traditional life policy provides a lump sum benefit upon the diagnosis of specific severe diseases?
questions.explanation: A Critical Illness policy or rider pays a guaranteed lump sum immediately upon the confirmed diagnosis of a specified severe illness (like cancer or heart attack).

questions.text: When is an 'Age Admission' strictly necessary?
options: It must be done before the settlement of any claim, if not done at the proposal stage
questions.explanation: While it's best to admit age at the start, an insurance company will not pay a maturity or death claim until standard proof of age is submitted and the age is formally 'admitted'.

questions.text: In the event of an unnatural death (e.g., accident or murder), which documents are mandatory for a claim?
options: FIR, Police Inquest Report, and Post-Mortem Report
A letter from the village head
questions.explanation: For unnatural deaths, police and medical investigations are legally required. Insurers mandate the FIR and Post-Mortem reports to confirm the exact cause and rule out foul play or exclusions.

questions.text: What does a 'Participating' policy mean?
options: The policyholder gets a free gift
The policyholder participates in the surplus profits of the insurance company through bonuses
The policyholder must participate in company meetings
questions.explanation: Participating (or With-Profits) policies are eligible to receive periodic bonuses declared from the insurer's valuation surplus.

questions.text: If an agent provides false or misleading information to a customer to make a sale, it is termed as:
questions.explanation: Mis-selling involves unethical practices like hiding facts, making false promises, or selling a product that does not fit the customer's financial needs.

questions.text: What happens if a policy is surrendered in the very first year?
options: Nothing is payable as the policy has not acquired any surrender value
The agent's commission is paid to the customer
questions.explanation: Traditional life policies typically require continuous premium payment for at least 2 or 3 years to acquire a cash surrender value. Quitting in the first year results in a total loss of the premium paid.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the 'Integrated Grievance Management System' (IGMS)?
options: To provide a central online repository and tracking mechanism for policyholder grievances across the industry
questions.explanation: IGMS is an IRDAI portal that allows customers to register complaints online and track their resolution status transparently.

questions.text: What is a 'Lien' in life insurance?
options: A clause that restricts or reduces the claim payout if death occurs within a specific initial period due to certain sub-standard risks
questions.explanation: A lien is a restrictive underwriting condition often placed on sub-standard lives (e.g., people with specific diseases), reducing the death benefit for the first few years.

questions.text: Can a minor buy a life insurance policy in their own name?
options: No, a minor cannot enter into a valid contract; a major (proposer) must buy it on the minor's life
Yes, if they have a bank account
Only if they are older than 15
questions.explanation: Under the Indian Contract Act, a minor is incompetent to contract. Hence, a parent or guardian acts as the proposer to buy the policy on the life of the minor.

questions.text: What does 'Mortgage Redemption' policy do?
options: Is a decreasing term insurance that pays off an outstanding home loan if the borrower dies
questions.explanation: It is a specialized decreasing term plan where the sum assured goes down over the years, matching the reducing balance of a housing loan.

questions.text: What is 'Adverse Selection' primarily caused by?
options: Information asymmetry, where a sick person hides their health condition to get a standard policy
questions.explanation: Adverse selection happens when individuals who know they have a high risk of claiming intentionally seek out insurance without revealing their true risk profile.

questions.text: According to the MWP Act, if a policyholder appoints his wife and children as beneficiaries, what happens to the policy proceeds if he goes bankrupt?
options: The proceeds are seized by the bank
The proceeds go to the government
The proceeds are protected as a trust and cannot be attached by his creditors
The proceeds are split between creditors and the family
questions.explanation: The core purpose of the Married Women's Property Act is to create an irrevocable trust, completely safeguarding the policy money from the husband's business debts or creditors.

questions.text: What does 'Rebating' mean in the insurance business?
options: Giving a legal discount on premium
An agent unethically sharing a portion of their commission with the customer to secure a sale
Returning the policy in the free look period
questions.explanation: Rebating is an illegal inducement. Section 41 of the Insurance Act strictly prohibits agents from offering their commission as a bribe to customers.

questions.text: Which document is generally issued to provide temporary insurance coverage while the formal policy document is being prepared?
options: Cover Note (more common in general insurance) or First Premium Receipt
questions.explanation: While 'Cover Notes' are primarily used in motor/general insurance, the First Premium Receipt acts as the equivalent temporary proof of risk acceptance in life insurance until the policy bond arrives.
